rename-and-remove-tracked-files.label: Rename and Remove Tracked Files
rename-and-remove-tracked-files.summary: Simplify community-project by renaming the schedule and removing the obsolete contact list.

sketch0.title: Tracked Paths Change with the Project

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.title: Rename the Tracked Schedule
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
Rename the tracked file by running:

```bash
git mv schedule.txt events.txt
```

This command renames the file in the working tree and stages the path change in one action. Git does not store a permanent rename object; when it compares snapshots, it recognizes that the same content moved from one path to another.

Leave the staged rename uncommitted so you can review it with the next project change.
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.hint: Use the Git-aware move for the tracked path.

help.concept: `git mv schedule.txt events.txt` renames the working file and stages the path change in one action. Git records the before-and-after snapshots rather than storing a special rename object.
help.hint_1: Keep the file in the repository root.

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.starterCode:
# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
git -C community-project init -q -b main
git -C community-project config --local user.name 'ZoeSkoul Learner'
git -C community-project config --local user.email learner@zoeskoul.local
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Start community project'
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0100
' > community-project/contacts.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-02T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-02T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Add community contacts'
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +

' > community-project/README.md

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.instructions: The project now calls its schedule `events.txt`. Rename tracked `schedule.txt` to `events.txt` with Git so the path change is staged.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you rename the tracked schedule to `events.txt`?

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.title: Remove the Obsolete Contact List
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
Remove the tracked file by running:

```bash
git rm contacts.txt
```

This command deletes `contacts.txt` from the working tree and stages its removal in one action.

Then inspect all staged path changes by running:

```bash
git diff --staged --name-status
```

The summary should show the removal of `contacts.txt` together with the staged rename from `schedule.txt` to `events.txt`. Git uses status letters such as `D` for a deleted file and `R` when it recognizes a likely rename.

Leave both staged changes uncommitted.
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.hint: Stage the tracked removal and review both structural changes.

help.concept: `git rm contacts.txt` deletes the working file and stages its removal. `git diff --staged --name-status` summarizes staged path changes with status letters such as `D` for deletion and `R` for a likely rename.
help.hint_2: The summary should contain the rename and the contact removal.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you stage the obsolete contact-list removal?

1.message: Did you review both staged path changes?

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.title: Commit the Structural Cleanup
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
Record the staged changes with the exact commit message:

```bash
git commit -m "Organize community project files"
```

Then run the short-status command to verify that no unfinished changes remain.

A clean result confirms that the rename to `events.txt` and the removal of `contacts.txt` were both recorded successfully.
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.hint: Commit the one-purpose path cleanup and verify the result.

help.concept: `git commit -m ...` records the staged repository shape. `git status --short` then confirms that the working tree and staging area match the new snapshot.
help.hint_2: The project should now contain only `README.md` and `events.txt`.

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.starterCode:
# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Organize community project files"
git status --short

printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/events.txt

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.instructions: Record the staged rename and removal with the message `Organize community project files`. Then confirm that short status is clean.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you record the structural cleanup?

1.message: Did you verify a clean state afterward?

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of git mv
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git mv` command?
single-choice-1.hint: `git mv` combines a working-tree path change with staging that change.

help.concept: Use Git-aware structural operations, review the staged paths, and record a cleanup without losing earlier versions.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To move a file without tracking
options.b: To rename a file and stage the change

single-choice-2.title: Effect of git rm
single-choice-2.prompt: What happens when you use the `git rm` command?
single-choice-2.hint: `git rm` removes the working file and prepares that deletion for the next commit.

help.concept: Use Git-aware structural operations, review the staged paths, and record a cleanup without losing earlier versions.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: The file is moved to a new location
options.b: The file is removed from the working tree and staged for deletion

multi-choice-1.title: Commands to Verify Staged Changes
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which commands can you use to verify staged changes before committing?
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Use Git-aware structural operations, review the staged paths, and record a cleanup without losing earlier versions.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

drag-reorder-1.title: Steps to Rename and Commit a File
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps to rename a file and commit the change using Git.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Use Git-aware structural operations, review the staged paths, and record a cleanup without losing earlier versions.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

tokens.t1: Use git mv to rename the file
tokens.t2: Check the status with git status
tokens.t3: Commit the change with git commit

sketch0.title: Rename a Tracked Path
sketch0.bodyMarkdown: The community organizers are preparing the file to hold cleanup days, planning meetings, and other activities. The existing cleanup entry remains its first event, but `events.txt` now describes the file’s growing purpose more accurately than `schedule.txt`.

sketch1.title: Remove a Tracked Path
sketch1.bodyMarkdown: The community organizers have moved contact information to another system, so `contacts.txt` is no longer maintained in this project. Keeping the outdated file could cause volunteers to rely on incorrect information.

sketch3.title: Record One Structural Purpose
sketch3.bodyMarkdown: The rename and removal serve the same project goal: simplifying how the community project is organized. Because both changes describe one clear update to the repository’s structure, they belong in the same focused commit.
